primary_diagnosis.name: Severe plaque psoriasis — acute explosive exacerbation (post-streptococcal, stress-triggered)

differential.evidence_basis: A 10-year history of the same condition, now with widespread thick well-demarcated erythematous plaques bearing dense silvery-white scale in a classic extensor (elbows/knees) plus scalp/trunk distribution, fissuring under tension — the hallmark plaque psoriasis pattern, dramatically flared.

A dermatological emergency to be actively excluded: it would produce generalised erythema over >90% of the body surface with impaired thermoregulation, fluid/protein loss and systemic instability. Must be screened by whether the redness is confluent over almost the whole body versus discrete plaques.
Another dermatological emergency: rapidly spreading sterile pustules on inflamed skin with fever and systemic upset. Screened by the presence/absence of pus-filled blisters and systemic features.

differential.diagnosis: secondary bacterial infection / staphylococcal sepsis of fissured skin
differential.evidence_basis: Fissured, bleeding skin is a portal for bacterial infection; must be screened by fever/rigors and purulent weeping/crusting.

A herpetic superinfection of diseased skin; would show monomorphic grouped vesicles or punched-out erosions with systemic fever — different morphology from thick silvery plaques, but worth a brief screen.

differential_progression.scoring_note: From the opening picture a severe plaque psoriasis flare should lead, but a competent responder actively keeps the dangerous mimics — erythrodermic psoriasis, generalised pustular psoriasis and secondary sepsis — on the list and screens them, without escalating the current stable flare prematurely.

differential.evidence_basis: Discrete well-demarcated plaques with normal skin between them (not confluent whole-body erythema), no pustules, no purulent weeping, no fever/rigors, systemically well — a severe but benign flare, coherent with the recent strep trigger and Koebner response.

Redness is in discrete plaques with intervening normal skin and does not cover the whole body; the patient is systemically well and normothermic — makes erythroderma very unlikely on the patient-obtainable data, though total BSA cannot be measured on video.
No pustules and no fever/systemic upset reported — makes generalised pustular psoriasis very unlikely.

No fever/rigors and no purulent weeping/crusting; the cracks bleed but are not infected on the patient-obtainable data. Remains the leading safety-net target because it can develop through fissured skin.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The targeted screen (whole-body redness? pustules? fever/rigors? purulent weeping?) down-weights the emergency mimics. The honest residual telehealth uncertainty — precise BSA, early pustulation, and early secondary infection cannot be verified on video — is handled by a prominent mandatory safety-net, NOT by escalating the current stable flare to ED.

differential.diagnosis: severe plaque psoriasis, acute explosive exacerbation
differential.evidence_basis: A severe-but-benign flare of known chronic plaque psoriasis, triggered by a recent streptococcal infection and stress. Managed on telehealth with intensive emollient, a potent topical corticosteroid to the worst fissured plaques, simple analgesia, an urgent (routine-urgent, not emergency) dermatology referral for systemic therapy, and prompt GP review — with a strong safety-net for the erythroderma/pustular/sepsis emergencies that cannot be fully excluded remotely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct endpoint is recognising a severe plaque psoriasis flare, giving flare management (emollient + potent topical steroid + analgesia), an appropriate T2 disposition (prompt GP + urgent dermatology referral), and a mandatory safety-net escalating to ED/000 for whole-body redness, high fever with rigors, spreading pustules, purulent weeping, or inability to stay warm. Over-triaging this stable flare straight to ED/000 is the scored defect.

red_flags.description: Erythrodermic psoriasis — generalised confluent redness/scaling over >90% of the body surface with impaired thermoregulation (inability to stay warm, or feeling burning hot), fluid/protein loss and systemic instability
red_flags.clinical_significance: A dermatological emergency: loss of the skin barrier over most of the body causes hypothermia or hyperthermia, dehydration, high-output cardiac strain and infection risk. Distinguished from a severe plaque flare by whether the redness is confluent over the whole body versus discrete plaques with normal intervening skin.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated erythroderma can be life-threatening through thermoregulatory failure, fluid loss and cardiovascular decompensation.

red_flags.description: Secondary bacterial infection / staphylococcal sepsis of the fissured skin — high fever, uncontrollable shivering/rigors, and thick yellow pus or purulent weeping from the cracks
red_flags.clinical_significance: Fissured, bleeding skin is a portal for bacterial entry; systemic infection can develop rapidly. Fever with rigors and purulent discharge are the discriminating features and were screened negative.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unrecognised skin sepsis can progress to severe systemic infection and shock.

red_flags.description: Generalised pustular psoriasis — crops of small sterile pus-filled blisters spreading rapidly over red skin, with fever and feeling unwell
red_flags.clinical_significance: A dermatological emergency requiring urgent in-person assessment and systemic therapy; distinguished from plaque psoriasis by the sudden appearance of pustules and systemic upset.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Generalised pustular psoriasis can cause systemic illness, dehydration and sepsis if not treated urgently.

red_flags.description: Eczema herpeticum — monomorphic clusters of small blisters or punched-out erosions with systemic fever, especially spreading over diseased skin
red_flags.clinical_significance: A herpetic superinfection needing urgent antiviral treatment; morphologically distinct from thick silvery plaques (monomorphic vesicles / punched-out erosions) and screened negative here.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Disseminated herpes simplex infection can be serious, particularly with extensive skin barrier disruption.

pathophysiology_summary: Plaque psoriasis is a chronic, immune-mediated inflammatory skin disease driven by activation of the IL-23/Th17 axis, producing keratinocyte hyperproliferation with markedly accelerated epidermal turnover (days rather than weeks). This generates the characteristic thick, well-demarcated erythematous plaques surmounted by dense silvery-white (micaceous) scale over extensor surfaces (elbows, knees), the scalp, sacrum/lower back and, in a flare, the trunk. Lifting a scale exposes dilated dermal papillary capillaries that bleed at pinpoints — the Auspitz sign. Streptococcal throat infection is a classic trigger (superantigen-driven T-cell activation) and psychosocial stress is a well-recognised precipitant, both consistent with this explosive 7-day flare. New plaques forming at sites of skin trauma (the recent forearm scratch) reflect the isomorphic Koebner phenomenon. In a severe flare the plaques become confluent and rigid; under mechanical tension across flexures they fissure into deep, painful, bleeding cracks, which — with the copious scale shedding — cause the functional immobility described. The key severity question is whether the flare has crossed into an emergency variant: erythrodermic psoriasis (confluent erythema >90% BSA with thermoregulatory/fluid failure) or generalised pustular psoriasis (sterile pustulation with systemic upset), and whether the fissured skin has become secondarily infected (staphylococcal sepsis). Here the redness is in discrete plaques with normal intervening skin, there are no pustules, no purulent discharge and no systemic features, so this is a severe but non-emergency plaque flare. Treatment restores the barrier (intensive greasy emollients soften and unlock the rigid, fissuring scale within 24-48 hours) and suppresses local inflammation (potent topical corticosteroid), while the underlying disease severity warrants specialist consideration of systemic immunomodulators or phototherapy.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Letting the severe pain, the continuous bleeding from the cracks, the immobility and the out-of-hours 'urgent' framing escalate a stable severe plaque flare straight to ED/000. The bleeding is superficial fissure/Auspitz bleeding, not haemorrhage, and the vitals and systemic screen are reassuring — the correct response is intensive flare management with a strong safety-net, not emergency transfer.

Reassuring and managing without screening for erythroderma (whole-body confluent redness / thermoregulatory failure), generalised pustular psoriasis (spreading pustules + fever) and secondary sepsis (fever/rigors + purulent weeping). These cannot be examined remotely, so they must be built into an explicit mandatory safety-net.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Under-treating the flare / dismissing to pure self-care
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating this as a trivial rash with only moisturiser and no medical review misses that a flare this severe needs a potent topical steroid, analgesia, and specialist referral for systemic therapy, and misses the mimic screen that only a clinician can complete in person.

Prescribing or advising oral/systemic corticosteroids for a psoriasis flare — abrupt systemic steroids can precipitate a rebound flare or convert plaque psoriasis into generalised pustular psoriasis, and are generally avoided. Escalation of systemic therapy is a dermatologist decision.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Wrong topical (steroid alone without emollient, or an ineffective agent)
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Relying on a topical steroid without the intensive greasy emollient that actually softens the rigid, fissuring scale and relieves the mechanical pain, or expecting the patient's small tube of calcipotriol/betamethasone to cover a now-vast surface area.

cannot_determine_via_chat: The Auspitz sign (pinpoint capillary bleeding on lifting a scale) — requires the clinician's hands
Accurate measurement of the percentage body-surface-area involved, needed to confirm or exclude erythroderma
Palpation of the plaques and fissures, and inspection for early pustulation or secondary infection
Nail examination for the pitting that supports the diagnosis
Clinician-measured vital signs (blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, calibrated temperature) — only patient-reported/self-measured values are available on telehealth
Skin swab / bloods to confirm or exclude secondary bacterial infection or streptococcal trigger
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: No emergency escalation of the current stable flare. Recognise a severe plaque psoriasis flare from history plus video appearance, advise intensive greasy emollient over the whole body plus a potent topical corticosteroid to the worst fissured plaques and simple analgesia, arrange an urgent (routine-urgent) dermatology referral for systemic therapy and a prompt GP review within a couple of days, and give a PROMINENT MANDATORY safety-net: attend the emergency department (or call 000 if severely unwell) if the redness spreads to cover almost the whole body, if a high fever with uncontrollable shivering develops, if crops of pus-filled blisters appear, if the cracks start weeping thick yellow pus, or if the patient cannot keep warm.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for benign flare management (emollient + potent topical steroid + analgesia are safe and appropriate presumptively). The real risks are (1) OVER-action — escalating a stable severe flare to ED/000 under the pressure of the pain, bleeding and out-of-hours framing; and (2) reassurance without the emergency-variant safety-net, given erythroderma, pustular psoriasis and secondary sepsis cannot be excluded remotely.
